data.title: Hong Kong seizes fake Gucci, Hermes, other goods worth HK$65 million during major sale
data.description:
Hong Kong customs has seized 120,000 suspected counterfeit items in a month-long operation that targeted cross-border transshipments during a major online shopping festival in mainland China, confiscating goods including fake Gucci handbags, Hermes shoes and latest models of the iPhone.
The Customs and Excise Department revealed on Friday that it conducted a special enforcement operation between October 20 and November 14, during which it solved a total of 29 cases and seized counterfeit goods worth an estimated HK$65 million (US$8.3 million).
“During the ‘Double 11’ shopping festival, with its massive transaction volume, criminals attempted to exploit the anonymity of online transactions to mix counterfeit goods with legitimate e-commerce products, hoping to smuggle them in or out of Hong Kong,” said Senior Inspector Liu Wai-kit of customs’ intellectual property transnational investigation division.
“Through intelligence analysis and risk management, and cooperating with large platforms handling cross-border e-commerce logistics, customs strengthened its inspections and checks.”
Double 11, or Singles’ Day, a major shopping festival in mainland China, falls on November 11 every year and is celebrated by many online shops and e-commerce platforms that roll out discounts to boost sales.
Liu added that the 29 cases involved multiple logistics centres, with preliminary investigations revealing that the seized items were meant to be shipped overseas via Hong Kong, including destinations in the Americas, Europe and the Middle East.
The seized goods covered many categories including clothing, footwear, electronic products and luxury goods.
Customs said the criminals exploited consumers’ desire to buy counterfeit branded goods at lower prices than the originals, and used the popular online shopping festival to push such bogus products.
Liu noted that the operation yielded a large number of counterfeit electronic products and accessories, including the newest models. He explained that compared with large home appliances, small items that carried a high value, such as wireless headphones, were easier to conceal and transport.
Fake luxury goods, such as Gucci handbags and Hermes and Dior shoes and wallets, could yield even greater profit margins.

This lucrative market drove criminals to continuously improve the quality and resemblance of counterfeit products to genuine articles, to meet the demand for high-end luxury goods, according to Liu.
Customs urged the local logistics industry to be vigilant regarding their clients’ goods. If employees suspected that the items they handled were counterfeit, they should file a report with law enforcement authorities.
The department also advised consumers to shop in stores or online shops with a good reputation. If they had doubts about the authenticity of goods, they should first check with the trademark holder or their authorised agent to avoid unnecessary losses.
Under the Trade Descriptions Ordinance, the maximum penalty for importing or exporting any goods using a forged trademark is a fine of HK$500,000 and five years’ imprisonment.

data.title: Nigerian Catholic school pupils abducted by gunmen in second attack in a week
data.description:
Armed gangs have abducted an unknown number of pupils from a Catholic school in central Nigeria, police and a government official said on Friday, in the second such incident in less than a week.
The school kidnappings and an attack on a church earlier in the week come weeks after US President Donald Trump threatened military action over what he described as the targeted killings of Nigeria’s Christians, a narrative rejected by the Nigerian government.
The Niger state government said it had “received with deep sadness the disturbing news of the kidnapping of pupils from St Mary’s School in Agwara local government area”.
“The exact number of abducted pupils is yet to be confirmed as security agencies continue to assess the situation,” Abubakar Usman, the state government secretary, said in a statement.
Local media broadcaster Arise TV said 52 schoolchildren were abducted.

Coming after gunmen stormed a secondary school in Kebbi state in northwestern Nigeria, abducting 25 schoolgirls early on Monday, Friday’s attack further raises alarm over security in Africa’s most populous country.
For years, heavily armed criminal gangs locally known as “bandits” have been intensifying attacks in rural areas of northwest and central Nigeria with little state presence, killing thousands and conducting kidnappings for ransom.
The state government said the school had defied orders to temporarily close all boarding schools in parts of the state following an intelligence report of an “increased threat level” in parts of northern Niger that border Kebbi.
Niger’s state police said its tactical units and the military were deployed to search for the pupils.
Police said they received a report at about 2am that “some armed bandits invaded St Mary’s Private (Catholic) Secondary School … and abducted a yet to be ascertained number of students from the school’s hostel”.
It said security agencies were “combing the forests with a view to rescue the abducted students”.
The federal government said this week that security forces had been placed on high alert.
Favoured tactic
In a separate attack on a church in western Nigeria on Tuesday, gunmen killed two people during a service that was recorded and broadcast online.
The gunmen also kidnapped 38 worshippers from the church and were demanding a ransom of 100 million naira (US$69,000) per worshipper, a church official said on Friday.
As the country grapples with security challenges on several fronts, hostage-taking has spiralled nationwide and become a favoured tactic of bandit gangs and jihadists.

Although bandits have no ideological leanings and are motivated by financial gains, their increasing alliance with jihadists from the northeast has been a source of concern for authorities and security analysts.
Jihadists have for 16 years been waging an insurrection in the northeast with the aim of establishing a caliphate.
The jihadist violence has killed more than 40,000 people and displaced around 2 million in the northeast since it erupted in 2019.
Additional reporting by Associated Press, Reuters

data.title: Chinese commerce minister calls for deeper US-China cooperation on trade
data.description:
Chinese Commerce Minister Wang Wentao called for China and the United States to make joint efforts to manage uncertainties and expand cooperation on trade during a meeting with the US ambassador to China, David Perdue, in Beijing on Thursday.
The two countries should maintain a “correct understanding” of the fact that China and the US must find ways to coexist and that win-win outcomes could be achieved, Wang said, according to a statement released by the commerce ministry after the meeting.
“Economic and trade ties should continue to serve as the ballast and engine of China-US relations, not as a stumbling block or a source of conflict,” Wang said, adding that the two sides should uphold the bilateral economic and trade consultation mechanism put in place earlier this year.
But Wang also expressed concerns over a range of US policies during the meeting, including its unilateral imposition of tariffs, export controls, restrictions on two-way investments, tougher visa screenings and restrictive clauses in agreements with third parties, according to the statement.
He also laid out China’s position in relation to the dispute over Nexperia – a Chinese-owned chip company that was seized by the Dutch government in late September – and exchanged views with Perdue on the implementation of measures agreed during recent bilateral economic and trade consultations, the statement said.
China was willing to work with the US to implement the consensus reached by Chinese President Xi Jinping and US President Donald Trump, Wang said.
After the two leaders met in late October, the two countries pledged to roll back tariffs on each other’s goods and suspend a series of other trade restrictions, with China agreeing to pause its expanded export controls over rare earth minerals.


In return, the US agreed to a year-long delay to its introduction of the “affiliate rule” – a measure announced in late September that extends US export restrictions to any entity at least 50 per cent owned by companies on a US trade blacklist.
The new rule appeared to play a direct role in the Nexperia saga, as the company’s Chinese owner – Wingtech – was added to the US entity list last year. The Hague moved to wrest control of Nexperia from Wingtech the day after Washington adopted the affiliate rule.
China and the Netherlands have been embroiled in a dispute over the company’s fate ever since, with Beijing imposing export restrictions on certain Nexperia components that threatened the chip supplies of European carmakers.
On Wednesday, the Dutch government announced it had lifted the Cold War-era order that allowed it to take control of Nexperia, a move that Beijing welcomed as a “first step in the right direction”.
During a public hearing on Thursday, US lawmakers argued that suspending the affiliate rule could make it easier for China to challenge America’s lead in semiconductor and artificial intelligence technology, as the one-year delay would give Chinese entities time to “create workarounds” to US export controls.
Wang, the Chinese commerce minister, said Beijing and Washington could bring greater stability and certainty to US-China relations and the global economy by working together to address challenges and broaden cooperation.

data.title: China is betting on the K visa as a magnet for top tech talent. Will it be enough?
data.description:
Computer science graduate Alain Saas is hoping a fourth time is the charm to make his China dream come true.
As a young man growing up in the small town of Selestat in eastern France, the distant Asian country seemed magical. But the dream has been elusive and trying to land a job in China has been anything but enchanting.
Over the past 15 years, Saas, who is now in his forties, has tried three times to find a suitable tech job in China. The attempts have been unsuccessful due to reasons ranging from poor timing and low pay to a lack of suitable opportunities in the industry.
But lately he is feeling more optimistic. As China rolls out friendlier visa offers and appears poised to make more technological leaps, Saas is ready to take his fourth shot.
“I am planning to double down on my search efforts next year after reaching HSK5 [working proficiency in Chinese],” Saas said. “The K Visa sounds good, though I may have passed the age limit … and given all that’s happening in technology in China, I’d be very excited to find something.”
China is also doubling down. Amid a global tech war, a race for talent is ramping up, and Beijing has committed to becoming a magnet for the world’s skilled immigrants in its economic and social development blueprint for the next five years.
In the proposals for the country’s 15th five-year plan released last month, the Central Committee of China’s ruling Communist Party announced it would “establish an immigration system for highly skilled talent” and “attract and cultivate outstanding talent from around the world”.
The pledge – an upgrade on the 14th five-year plan’s promise to merely “explore” tech immigration – came around the same time as last month’s launch of the K visa, a streamlined pathway targeting young professionals in majors of science, technology, engineering and mathematics (STEM).
Details of the K visa are still unknown, including the age limit, scope of scientific and technological fields or industries covered, the specific validity period and permitted length of stay, and residential requirements for visa holders.
According to census data, 845,000 foreign nationals lived in mainland China in 2020, accounting for 0.06 per cent of the total population. In contrast, in June this year, 52 million immigrants were living in the United States – about 15 per cent of that country’s population.


As the US imposes US$100,000 fees on new H-1B applicants under the administration of President Donald Trump, Beijing is positioning the K-visa as a lower-barrier alternative, requiring no employer sponsorship and providing potential fast-tracks to permanent residency, though few details are known.
The policy is the centrepiece of China’s broader tech ambitions: to dominate “new quality productive forces” – a phrase coined by President Xi Jinping to stress innovation-driven economic development as the country’s workforce ages and US export curbs bite into chip and AI tool supplies. The future hurdles for research and development have made foreign expertise essential to China’s goals.
In 2024, China had more than 10 million R&D personnel with the total number of “skilled talent” exceeding 220 million, according to official data. The huge reservoir of talent combined with billions of dollars in state funding has sparked a series of technological advances over the past decade, most visibly in green energy, electric vehicles and artificial intelligence.
However, a shortage of high-level talent – such as top tier scientists – remains an obstacle that must be addressed, according to Li Zuojun, a researcher with the Development Research Centre of the State Council.
China should “expand international openness and cooperation in talent”, Li wrote in the official China Economic Times earlier this month. The measures should include attracting global intellectual resources to help China innovate, improving hi-tech talent immigration policies, simplifying work permits and residence visas and creating a “liveable and workable environment” for talent development, he wrote.
However, insiders have warned that China’s immigration apparatus is not ready yet.
David Chew, a Malaysian satellite-tech founder in his mid-thirties who is fluent in Chinese, embodies the ideal candidate. Several years ago, he chose to start his business in Japan after completing his education there and having worked for Japanese companies. “The K visa is a good start,” he said, “but the local ecosystem is not ready to support it”.
To rival the US, China needs “a standardised points system, multi-year certainty, flat tax rates and seed capital,” Chew said.
Without them, he said talent is deterred by having to choose geopolitical sides. “Coming to China to a certain extent means saying bye to the Western world – like picking Huawei over iPhone,” he said.
Chew predicted early winners: foreigners already in China on student visas, able to switch seamlessly, and “with experience already in place”. A second wave, he added, could come via entrepreneurship programmes offering English navigation and start-up grants.
In aerospace, the gap between rhetoric and reality has become stark. An executive at a private aviation start-up in Beijing, who declined to be named so he could comment freely, acknowledged there was demand but cited paralysing secrecy.
“We’re too closed-off; many units are classified,” he said. “We need international exchanges, but we do not dare recruit foreigners out of fear of leaks.”
In recent years, Beijing has tightened scrutiny over businesses by expanding national security laws, which now cover a broader range of activities and apply to both domestic and foreign companies.
Even non-state firms shun outsiders socially and professionally, the executive said. Domestic workers have largely sufficed unless foreigners offer R&D skills that cannot be duplicated. The job market’s cutthroat dynamic means companies are not “desperate enough to take risks”, he said.
China’s urban jobless rate among those aged 16 to 24 eased slightly to 17.7 per cent in September after climbing to 18.9 per cent in August – a 20-month high in the sagging economy.
Joseph Chamie, a demographer and former director of the United Nations’ Population Division, said that while many governments have attempted to limit immigration, reduce the sizes of their foreign-born populations and address domestic unemployment concerns, countries were also seeking to attract hi-tech migrants and benefit from “brain gains”.
China’s K visa programme stands in “stark contrast” to the US move to raise fees for H-1B visas, he said. However, for many hi-tech and highly educated potential migrants, the US remains a more attractive destination than China for various reasons, including language, a culture of immigration, long-term opportunities and lifestyle, Chamie added.
“Nevertheless, China’s increasing efforts to attract STEM talented migrants are likely to achieve some success especially if the US makes it more costly and difficult for foreign skilled migrants,” he said, adding China was likely to achieve “some limited success” in attracting such talent.
Sourabh Gupta, a senior policy specialist with the Institute for China-America Studies in Washington, said: “The intentions are certainly good; whether the policy turns out to be a success is a different matter, and I lean on the sceptical side of things in this regard.”
“By shooting for young, ambitious talent, the opportunity to build long-term collaborative relationships that span borders has now opened up. Done right, the K visa will not only enrich China’s science and technology ecosystem but could perhaps even incubate a rare few next-generation tech superstars of non-Chinese origin,” Gupta said. “It could also potentially transform major Chinese cities into hubs of cosmopolitanism.”
“But the barriers to success are high too. The ‘Great Firewall’ [blocked internet access to the outside world], the ‘996’ work-life balance, the relative lack of English proficiency or even signage in Chinese cities, etc are only the most immediate,” Gupta cautioned.
“On unconventional immigration initiatives such as the K visa, the government needs to adopt a liberal mindset of ‘setting it free’ … Whether this is even possible within the Chinese system remains to be seen,” he said. “There must be trust, too, among foreign STEM talent that their inventions and discoveries will be adequately protected,” he said.
It is not easy for a foreigner to get a visa to live in China. The application process is complex, requiring navigation of local regulations and submission of extensive documentation, including employment contracts and proof of qualifications, all of which are then thoroughly scrutinised by the authorities.
Under China’s immigration regulations, foreigners who have held senior positions – such as deputy general manager or an equivalent role – for at least four consecutive years and have lived in China for at least three years with a good tax record are eligible to apply for permanent residency.
Those who have made significant and outstanding contributions to China or who are urgently needed by the country are also eligible.
In August, Beijing announced a new category of K visas for young science and technology talent from overseas, which took effect on October 1. Eligible applicants include STEM graduates from recognised universities or research institutions worldwide who hold at least a bachelor’s degree or young professionals engaged in relevant education or research at such institutions.
There is no requirement for a Chinese employer or inviter at the application stage. Holders of K visas can be engaged in education, research, cultural exchange, entrepreneurship or business in China.
Ruby Osman, a policy adviser and China expert at the Tony Blair Institute for Global Change, said visas are only one of the obstacles faced by foreign talent: increasing research openness, providing a clearer regulatory environment and tackling language barriers will be essential if China is to become an attractive long-term talent destination.
There is also a domestic trade-off. The “opening up” policy in immigration is politically delicate. Bringing in new talent is a tough sell when China’s own STEM graduates are already struggling to find jobs, Osman said.
“That means Beijing will need to walk a fine line – promoting the K visa as proof of openness abroad while reassuring audiences at home that any impact will be carefully controlled,” Osman said.
The K visa is a step in the right direction but more details on its specific requirements and clarity on permitted activities would be key in determining its success, according to Ioana Kraft, chair of the human resources working group at the European Union Chamber of Commerce in China.
She said that hiring local Chinese workers had become an established practice for Western companies operating in China in recent years.
“Hiring foreign nationals has been deprioritised by many chamber members, a large percentage of whom have taken the decision to localise their workforce,” Kraft said.
“This is partly due to compensation packages for foreign nationals being higher, but also a reflection of the fact that Chinese staff tend to have a better understanding of the China market, while also having a language advantage,” she added.
Back in 2009, Alain Saas’ first attempt to make it to China fizzled after the salary offers did not meet his expectations. In 2015, a visa issue prevented him from attending a face-to-face interview. Then, a global tech giant that had posted a position downsized its China operations. These days, he says his job searches have turned up fewer openings.
“There seem to be no more or very few foreign companies hiring foreigners, few job postings in English, and in general the feeling I get is that the hiring of ‘foreign talent’ is mostly geared towards Chinese returning from studying or living abroad,” he said.
“In G7 countries all jobs are easy to find on [social media], in English, which is very convenient, but China is unfortunately disconnected from that.”
“So I am trying to slowly grow a network in China and hoping someone will eventually be able to connect me to an opportunity. While still working hard on my Chinese, hoping it will eventually pay off,” he said.

data.title: Does a faith-based education really contribute to academic excellence in Hong Kong?
data.description:
Long before public and private school systems existed, the world’s first classrooms were almost all faith-based – from European monasteries in the Middle Ages to schools that integrated religion with secular learning throughout the Reformation and Enlightenment.
Today, faith-based schools – be they Catholic, Protestant, Buddhist, Jewish, Muslim or otherwise – continue to offer rigorous academics alongside moral and spiritual instruction, laying a foundation for learning that nurtures both intellectual growth and the development of strong character. This distinctive blend is what makes these schools valuable in today’s diverse and competitive educational landscape.
While some parents are wary, others are curious: what are the pros and cons of a religious education for students, and how does such instruction affect their future and academic excellence? Are faith-based schools better at instilling discipline and purpose in students, or do they tend to insulate children from the wider world?

According to Dr Melissa Giglio, a clinical psychologist specialising in child, adolescent and family therapy at Central Health in Hong Kong, “The advantage of religious schools is that there is an emphasis on promoting spiritual understanding, consciousness, community and care towards others. These are values that we try to instil in children regardless of their spiritual affiliation,” she says.
“One of the primary benefits [of attending a faith-based school] is the integration of faith-based values into the curriculum,” says Dr Cora Hui, head of Christian Alliance P.C. Lau Memorial International School (CAPCL). “Instilling Biblical values and a moral framework in our students shapes their character and prepares them to be influential role models in their communities.”
At Carmel School Hong Kong, “students are provided with a set of values and a moral framework rooted in the Old Testament and Jewish faith, which gently guides them to understand, appreciate and practise justice, righteousness, kindness and compassion,” says Rachel Friedmann, principal. “Values such as achdut [‘unity’ in Hebrew] foster collaboration, empathy and mutual support among students, and teach them the importance of working together to overcome challenges.”

Besides emphasising values and moral frameworks, faith-based schools also expose students to religious rituals, routines and traditions that can promote cultural learning and understanding.
Nitsan Segev-Levi, head of elementary at Carmel, explains how the school’s curriculum is supplemented by experiential, faith-based learning. “Through [experiences such as] assembly guest speakers, Jewish festival celebrations and co-curricular activities focused on prayer, good deeds and asking for forgiveness, [students learn] the tenets of the Jewish practice and morality rooted in a respect for all,” she says.
Students don’t have to be religious to benefit from faith-based learning. At Anfield School, a Catholic institution, “inclusivity is a non-negotiable”, says executive head teacher Elise Rees. “We welcome families of all beliefs and teach from a Catholic ethos that emphasises dignity, service and respect. Religious activities are age-appropriate and focused on values such as kindness and stewardship. Students of other faiths are respected participants, with sensitivity to parents’ wishes.”

No matter the religion, faith-based schools “emphasise the development of positive character traits that align with being a compassionate, empathetic, generous and caring individual”, Giglio says. “They promote service to others and, by sharing time and resources to help those in need, focus on others in the greater community rather than solely on themselves.”
A faith-based school is inherently a community, so naturally there tends to be a strong emphasis on community outreach and giving back to others.
“Students are encouraged to develop a sense of compassion, empathy and social responsibility from a young age, and community service is an integral part of our children’s education and curriculum,” Rees says. “Through various initiatives such as charity drives and community outreach projects across all campuses, they learn the importance of giving back to society and caring for the vulnerable.”

At CAPCL, students take part in initiatives that go beyond the campus, including participating in Box of Hope by packing boxes of essential supplies and Christmas gifts for children living in poverty in Hong Kong and Southeast Asia. They also bring joy and warmth to the elderly by distributing mooncakes over the Mid-Autumn Festival and knitting scarves for them in the cold winter months.
“Christian schools encourage students to develop a serving heart, motivating them to engage in acts of kindness and service within the community,” Hui says. “This commitment to serving others guides children to look beyond themselves and consider the needs of those around them.”
At Carmel, community service opportunities extend beyond Hong Kong to Cambodia, Israel, Poland and other locations worldwide. “This emphasis on tikkun olam [“repairing the world”] develops students’ sense of social responsibility and global citizenship, preparing them to be leaders who contribute meaningfully to both the Jewish and wider world,” Friedmann says.

Besides their strong emphasis on character development, community service and leadership, some faith-based schools also report positive impacts on students’ academic performance thanks to their ethos.
“Placing your child in a faith school guides and enhances all aspects of personal growth and development in a holistic way,” Friedmann says.
“It also leads to higher academic outcomes: the UK’s 2025 Sunday Times Parent Power guide lists the top 10 comprehensive state schools, with seven of them [being] faith schools.”
Whether there is a direct correlation between faith-based education and higher academic achievement, such institutions generally place greater emphasis on discipline and homework, a positive school environment with high expectations, and a strong connection between school, home and community.
Faith-based schools promote service to others and focus on the greater community
Dr Melissa Giglio
“Our faith base provides our students with a clear, practical understanding of right and wrong within a community framework – both within our school as well as the global Jewish and wider communities,” Friedmann states.
“This foundation of identity security allows students to approach academic challenges with confidence and to engage respectfully with diverse perspectives in our international environment.”
Says Hui, “We recognise that each child is created with inherent value and potential, and students are encouraged to celebrate and develop their unique strengths and talents. By providing opportunities for self-discovery and personal growth, students explore their interests, take risks and learn from their experiences.”

Despite the advantages faith-based schools present, Giglio does not see a direct correlation between religion and academic excellence. “A person can be a strong student without strong religious beliefs, and someone who is weaker academically can have strong spiritual beliefs.”
While there may not be a definitive link between faith and higher grades, many parents and alumni point to a different kind of report card – one written in the small, daily acts of character. It’s found in the students who regularly volunteer at their local community centre, their natural curiosity and cultural understanding of the world, and their emphasis on teamwork over rivalry. Besides academic excellence, the value of a faith-based education often unfolds in the choices students make later in life – as members of the community, colleagues and global citizens.
Ultimately, faith-based schools aim to cultivate high-performing students who are grounded, compassionate and equipped with a strong moral compass for the journey ahead. And that’s certainly something to believe in.

data.title: Tech war: Huawei to open-source software tool to optimise AI chip use
data.description:
Huawei Technologies on Friday introduced open-source software that it claimed can significantly improve the utilisation rate of artificial intelligence chips, marking the latest effort by Chinese companies to achieve world-class AI training capabilities even without access to the latest Nvidia processors.
Flex:ai, which pools and orchestrates processors to squeeze out more performance, came as part of Huawei’s effort to develop a self-sufficient compute ecosystem by overcoming China’s shortcomings in individual chipsets.
Built on an open-sourced platform called Kubernetes, the software is an orchestration system for graphics processing units (GPUs), neural processing units (NPUs) and other accelerators from different chipmakers. It could slice a single card into multiple virtualised computing units, allowing several AI workloads to run in parallel, according to Huawei.
The tool could enhance the processor utilisation rate by 30 per cent on average, the company said. A smart scheduler called Hi Scheduler handles GPU and NPU allocation across the cluster. The system can also pool idle processors from different nodes and redistribute their power to AI tasks.
Kubernetes – now a backbone of AI infrastructure – is an open-source system that coordinates large fleets of containerised applications. Containerisation is a technique that makes it easy to deploy and scale AI models across different servers and GPU clusters.
“Smaller tasks rarely use up a single card’s capability, while bigger ones can’t be handled by just one, and parallel tasks make compute management harder,” said Zhou Yuefeng, vice-president and head of Huawei’s data storage product line, at a launch event on Friday at the company’s Lianqiuhu campus in Shanghai.
Flex:ai was designed to improve usage of compute resources and was part of Huawei’s effort to speed up the “democratisation of AI” by unleashing the potential of AI infrastructure and open sourcing, he said.
“Getting AI to work in real-world settings is tough,” Zhou said. “Huawei’s engineers can’t do it alone.”


Flex:ai will be open-sourced through Huawei’s ModelEngine developer community.
Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Xian Jiaotong University and Xiamen University collaborated on the project.
The software comes as Chinese tech firms increasingly turn to software optimisation to offset limited access to the most advanced chips, and as the AI boom creates sprawling clusters of accelerators that need smarter, more automated ways to run efficiently.
Huawei’s new release resembles offerings from Run:AI, a Tel Aviv start-up acquired by Nvidia for US$700 million in 2024, known for orchestrating AI workloads across large GPU clusters.
In August, Huawei also unveiled Unified Cache Manager (UCM), a software tool that speeds up AI inference by assigning data across different memory tiers based on latency needs, which could help reduce China’s reliance on foreign-made high-bandwidth memory chips.

data.title: Why China Miao ethnic group shows love by biting each other and gentle bites mean unloving
data.description:
A bizarre ancient courtship ritual practised by some of China’s ethnic groups involved a test of love where only a deep and painful bite was seen as a true expression of affection.
This practice, known as yao shou ding qing, or “biting the hand to seal the love,” is a traditional courtship ritual of the Miao ethnic group in Hainan province, located at the nation’s southernmost point.
Officially recognised as a provincial-level intangible cultural heritage since 2017, it reflects the Miao philosophy that “everything leaves a trace.”
The ritual typically takes place under evergreen trees, such as betel palm trees, which symbolise enduring love.

During festivals like the third day of the third lunar month, a traditional Miao holiday for ancestral worship, young men and women gather under betel trees or on grassy hillsides to sing folk songs that express their emotions and hopes of finding a soulmate.
When a young man falls in love, he may serenade a girl at night using a nose flute or tree leaves to produce soft, flowing melodies.
If the woman is uninterested or already has a partner, she will respond with a light, polite bite, which indicates a lack of romantic interest and signals rejection.
However, if the feeling is mutual and the young woman is willing to accept the man’s affection, she will bite his hand firmly – sometimes even hard enough to draw blood.

This intense and deliberate bite serves as a powerful symbol of her acceptance and deep emotional commitment.
Following this heartfelt expression of love, the couple will seal their commitment through a symbolic exchange: the man typically gives the woman a silver ring or a pair of silver earrings, while the woman reciprocates with handcrafted everyday items, such as a bamboo hat or a woven basket.
This tradition is beautifully encapsulated in the lyrics of a folk song, “Brother, give me your hand and let me leave a bite mark. The deeper the mark, the deeper our love. The green mountains may never grow old, and the mark will remain – seeing the bite mark is like seeing you again.”

The couple may then proceed to a formal engagement ceremony known among the Miao people as “eating chicken,” during which the bride’s family slaughters a chicken to host the groom’s family, symbolising their approval of the marriage.
Notably, a similar custom exists among China’s Yao ethnic minority, though with reversed roles: to affirm a relationship, the young man bites the woman’s arm as a symbol of loyalty and devotion.
However, the force of the bite must be carefully controlled – a bite that is too light may be seen as a lack of genuine affection, while a bite that is too strong could leave a mark, which, if discovered, may become a source of ridicule.

data.title: Is Beijing’s spat with Japan sending a message to US and allies over Taiwan?
data.description:
Beijing’s recent fury directed towards Japan is hammering home a point on Taiwan to the wider world, especially the United States and its allies, analysts in mainland China have suggested.
Earlier this month, Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said a conflict in the Taiwan Strait might be a “survival-threatening situation” that would justify military intervention.
The comments appeared to challenge Japan’s policy of strategic ambiguity about how it would respond to any crisis – something Beijing said would undermine the post-war international order.
Beijing warned Tokyo to brace for “all the consequences” before unleashing a series of retaliatory measures this week, while suggesting more might follow.
This stance could be a “multilayered” strategic signal, according to Chen Yang, director of the Japan Research Centre at the Beijing-based think tank Haiyi Institute.
“[It] is not only aimed at Japan but also a reminder to the United States and its system of allies – including Australia and South Korea – as well as a broader range of Asia-Pacific nations: stay prudent on the so-called Taiwan contingency,” he said.
Beijing’s response “creates a ‘demonstration effect’ in the regional strategic context: it conveys a warning to all countries that might be drawn into the Taiwan situation but have yet to take an explicit stance”, according to Chen Hong, director of the Australian Studies Centre at East China Normal University in Shanghai.
He said Beijing’s message included “do not position yourselves on the front lines of confrontation with China”.
“Overall, the approach can function both to uphold [Beijing’s] position on Taiwan and to issue an early warning against the potential spreading of intervening roles within the network of US Asia-Pacific allies.”
Takaichi’s comment – which she said was “hypothetical” but has refused to retract – made her the first Japanese leader since World War II to directly state that the country’s troops might intervene in a conflict over Taiwan.
Official media in mainland China accused her of displaying an “ambition” to militarily intervene and of making a “threat of force” against Beijing – both unprecedented since Japan’s defeat in 1945.
Japan’s post-war constitution renounces the use of force to settle international disputes and says its military can only be used for self-defence.
Earlier this year, the Financial Times reported that the US was pressing Japan and Australia to make clear what role they would play in the event of a war over the island.
According to the report, the Japanese defence ministry responded that it was “difficult to answer” a hypothetical question of that nature.


A recent report by the Rand Corporation think tank said the two countries could play a “core” role in US-led sanctions designed to deter an attack on Taiwan.
Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said last month that his country supported “the status quo when it comes to Taiwan”.
“We have a bipartisan position for the one-China policy, but we also don’t want to see any unilateral action,” he added.
Takaichi’s remarks could be read as a “belated” open response to the US request and a signal Japan was willing to align with Washington on the issue, according to Chen from Haiyi.
“[Beijing’s] warning to Japan is ringing alarm bells for the American alliance system, making other US allies such as Australia and South Korea clearly recognise the huge cost of following suit in intervening,” he said.
“That also serves as an indirect warning to the US … [that] any attempt to interfere in China’s internal affairs by leveraging alliance power will be countered.”
He also said that Beijing’s recent actions were sending a clear message to the international community about “respecting each country’s sovereignty and territorial integrity”.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary.
Most countries – including the US, Japan and other American allies – do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
On Thursday, George Glass, the US ambassador to Japan, said Washington was supporting Takaichi in the row with Beijing.
“I just want to say directly from the president and from myself and from the embassy … we have her back,” Glass said, according to Kyodo News.

Last week, the US approved the first arms sales to Taiwan since Donald Trump returned to the White House, triggering strong condemnation from Beijing.
Earlier this month, the Chinese embassy in Wellington posted the full text of the 1972 joint communique issued when New Zealand switched diplomatic recognition from Taipei to Beijing – which “acknowledged” Beijing’s one-China policy – in an apparent protest about a group of MPs attending a reception at Taiwan’s de facto embassy.
Also last week, China’s ambassador to South Korea Dai Bing cautioned against “changing the strategic goals” of the alliance between Seoul and Washington, and warned them “not to play with fire” on Taiwan.
Beijing describes Taiwan as a “core interest”, where its official positions are not subject to negotiation or compromise.
“Advancing the cause of national reunification” ranked as a priority in the framework for the five-year plan adopted by the Communist Party’s Central Committee last month.

data.title: Philippines’ Marcos orders arrest of Zaldy Co, 17 others in flood control scandal
data.description:
Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr has confirmed that an arrest warrant has been issued for former lawmaker Zaldy Co and 17 others accused of involvement in anomalous infrastructure projects.
The move marks the first major enforcement action since authorities began probing so-called “ghost” flood control projects, referring to substandard or non-existent works now at the centre of a sprawling corruption scandal.
Marcos announced the development in a video posted to his social media accounts on Friday, saying the warrants covered Co – the former House appropriations committee chair – as well as officials from the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) and executives of Sunwest Corporation, a construction firm Co co-founded.
“There is already a warrant of arrest against Zaldy Co and 17 others for DPWH and Sunwest Corporation,” Marcos said, urging law enforcement agencies to act swiftly. “Arrest them. Make sure that this will be enforced fast and correctly. The wheels of justice are turning.”
The president added that he had directed the DPWH and the Independent Commission for Infrastructure (ICI) to turn over all documents related to alleged irregularities involving Co and former House speaker Martin Romualdez to the Office of the Ombudsman.
“If any evidence is found, maybe a case of plunder or corruption or indirect bribery will be filed,” he said.
The Ombudsman welcomed the arrest warrants and called on authorities to “execute these warrants without delay”, stressing that the accused should be brought “before the bar of justice”.
The charges – including corruption and malversation – were formally filed on Wednesday after investigators from the ICI and DPWH submitted evidence linking Co and the other suspects to inflated, substandard or non-implemented flood control projects.
Co was among the first lawmakers implicated when the scandal erupted in July, after severe flooding paralysed parts of Manila and surrounding provinces.
Sunwest had secured billions of pesos in public works contracts during Co’s term as appropriations chair from 2022 until January 2025, though the former congressman has said he divested from the firm in 2019.
Co left the Philippines in July and his whereabouts remain unknown. In a series of video statements released on Friday, he accused Marcos and Romualdez of benefiting from 56 billion pesos in alleged kickbacks and claimed the scandal involved more than 100 billion pesos in budget insertions. He also alleged that Romualdez had threatened to kill him should he return to the country.
Both Marcos and Romualdez have denied the accusations, with the president urging Co to return and testify under oath.

data.title: SinoLand’s One Park Place sells out 150 units within hours on keen pricing
data.description:
One Park Place in Yau Tong sold out all 150 units in its first batch within half a day at its launch, as developer Sino Land used aggressive pricing to tap into returning demand in Hong Kong’s new home market.
The project, developed by Sino Land together with CSI Properties and MTR Corporation, opened sales on Thursday and saw all flats taken up by the afternoon, the developer said.
The first batch comprised 27 one-bedroom, 120 two-bedroom and three three-bedroom units, ranging from 293 to 578 sq ft.
Among the 150 units, the cheapest home – a one-bedroom unit – was priced at HK$4.27 million (US$550,000) after discounts, or HK$14,589 per square foot. The lowest price per square foot was HK$13,513 for a two-bedroom unit just under HK$5 million.
Sino Land executive director Victor Tin Siu-un attributed the quick sale to the prime location and aggressive pricing, which he said was at about a nine-year low for urban homes. The first batch averaged HK$14,701 per square foot after discounts.
One Park Place attracted more than 10 big-ticket buyers, reflecting resilient demand and optimism among investors, Tin said. He added that Sino Land could raise prices and release more units for sale soon.
Sammy Po Siu-ming, senior director at Midland Realty, said that among the buyers purchasing units for their own use, 80 per cent of them were young clients.
The first batch received more than 4,500 cheques ahead of the launch, making it more than 30 times oversubscribed.
Sino Land unveiled the first price list of One Park Place on November 11, involving 150 units at a discounted average price of HK$14,588 per square foot, 19 per cent cheaper than the nearby Chill Residence project launched three years ago.
On Monday, the developer disclosed prices for a further 78 units at a discounted average of HK$14,988 per square foot, 2.7 per cent higher than the previous list.
Located at 8 Ko Nga Lane in Yau Tong, One Park Place comprises 748 units in total, with one-bedroom and two-bedroom units accounting for over 80 per cent of the development. It is next to Yau Tong MTR Station, according to the official website.
Residential market sentiment improved since April on hopes that the US Federal Reserve would restart its interest rate-cut cycle and amid a rally in local stocks. The official price index rose for the fourth consecutive month in September, bringing the cumulative gain to 1.1 per cent in the first nine months of the year.
Cautious pricing strategies had also boosted transactions in the primary market, resulting in about 1,143 deals as of Wednesday this month, data from Hong Kong Property Services showed.
It marked the 10th straight month in which more than 1,000 new homes changed hands per month, the longest streak since 2013.
Developers were also preparing to roll out more units, with the 525-unit Double Coast III in Kai Tak uploading its prospectus on Thursday afternoon. Leading developer Wheelock Properties said the first price list was expected to be disclosed soon.
